meta.decision: Should Excalidraw build a paid realtime-collaboration backend (own infra) now, instead of continuing to rely on the open-source excalidraw-room and third-party embeds?
meta.temporalNotes: Excalidraw+ subscription growth has been flat for two quarters. Competitor tldraw just raised a $10M Series A and is shipping a realtime collaboration SDK aggressively. Internal team planning meeting on the collaboration roadmap is in 2 days. A conference talk on our collaboration story has a submission deadline in 3 weeks. The next quarterly board update covering the monetization plan is in 6 weeks.

contextInput.businessContextText: Excalidraw is a bootstrapped, open-source (MIT-licensed) virtual collaborative whiteboard for hand-drawn-style diagrams, launched in 2020 by former Meta engineer Christopher Chedeau and now run by a small team (~6 people) headquartered in Brno, Czech Republic. It operates a freemium model: the core app is free forever, while Excalidraw+ is a paid, seat-based cloud tier (~$6/user/month billed yearly, $7 monthly) targeting teams and enterprises with cloud workspaces, access management, presentations, comments, voice hangouts, and AI features. Its differentiation is deliberate simplicity, a sketch-like aesthetic, privacy-first end-to-end encryption, and no-signup access—serving individual developers plus small-to-mid-market product, engineering, and business teams who want quick informal diagramming without enterprise complexity. It competes with tldraw (an open-source infinite-canvas SDK for developers, VC-backed with ~$14M raised), Figma FigJam (design-team whiteboard bundled into Figma seats), and Miro (the enterprise category leader with ~90-100M users, ~$665M ARR and a $17.5B valuation). Key growth constraints are converting free/open-source users into paid Excalidraw+ subscribers, limited enterprise support capacity given the tiny team, and meeting enterprise auditability/reliability/security demands (addressed via SOC 2 Type II compliance and a DPA) against far larger, better-funded rivals.
contextInput.technicalContextText: Excalidraw is a TypeScript monorepo (Yarn workspaces) whose flagship deliverable is a React 19 npm component library, plus a reference web app (excalidraw-app) hosting excalidraw.com. It's a client-heavy, canvas-based SPA built with Vite (PWA plugin, offline service worker) and split into internal packages (common, element, math, excalidraw, utils, fractional-indexing, laser-pointer). The hosted app has no traditional backend of its own: real-time collaboration runs over Socket.IO with end-to-end encryption, and scene/file persistence uses Firebase Firestore + Firebase Storage, with local-first storage via idb-keyval. State is managed with Jotai; deployment is via Vercel for the web app and a multi-stage Docker/nginx image for self-hosting. Observability is limited to Sentry error tracking; CI (GitHub Actions) runs Vitest tests, ESLint, Prettier, typechecking, size-limit, and locale coverage.
contextInput.temporalContextText: There is a near-term internal team planning meeting on the collaboration roadmap in 2 days, making it the most immediate time pressure. A conference talk submission deadline on the collaboration story falls in 3 weeks, and the next quarterly board update covering the monetization plan is in 6 weeks. Competitive pressure from tldraw's recent funding and aggressive SDK shipping adds urgency to these decisions.
contextInput.decisionText: Should Excalidraw build a paid realtime-collaboration backend (own infra) now, instead of continuing to rely on the open-source excalidraw-room and third-party embeds?

technical.summary: Excalidraw is a TypeScript monorepo (Yarn workspaces) whose flagship deliverable is a React 19 npm component library, plus a reference web app (excalidraw-app) hosting excalidraw.com. It's a client-heavy, canvas-based SPA built with Vite (PWA plugin, offline service worker) and split into internal packages (common, element, math, excalidraw, utils, fractional-indexing, laser-pointer). The hosted app has no traditional backend of its own: real-time collaboration runs over Socket.IO with end-to-end encryption, and scene/file persistence uses Firebase Firestore + Firebase Storage, with local-first storage via idb-keyval. State is managed with Jotai; deployment is via Vercel for the web app and a multi-stage Docker/nginx image for self-hosting. Observability is limited to Sentry error tracking; CI (GitHub Actions) runs Vitest tests, ESLint, Prettier, typechecking, size-limit, and locale coverage.

facts.label: Language & framework
facts.value: TypeScript 5.9 + React 19, built with Vite 5; canvas-based whiteboard SPA

facts.label: Architecture
facts.value: Yarn workspaces monorepo: excalidraw-app + internal packages (common, element, math, excalidraw, utils, fractional-indexing, laser-pointer); library published as @excalidraw/excalidraw npm package

facts.label: Internal packages
facts.value: Modular design with separate packages: common, element, excalidraw, fractional-indexing, laser-pointer, math, utils

facts.label: Persistence & backend
facts.value: No custom backend; scenes stored in Firebase Firestore, files in Firebase Storage, both end-to-end encrypted client-side

facts.label: Real-time collaboration
facts.value: socket.io-client 4.7.2 used for real-time collab; local-first with idb-keyval and jotai for state

facts.label: Deployment (self-host)
facts.value: Multi-stage Dockerfile building with Node 24 and serving static build via nginx-alpine; docker-compose exposes port 3000:80

facts.label: Deployment (hosted)
facts.value: Vercel deploys excalidraw-app/build with CORS/security headers and host-based redirects; install via yarn

facts.label: Observability
facts.value: Sentry browser error tracking (@sentry/browser 9.0.1); tracking toggled via VITE_APP env flags, disabled in Docker builds

facts.label: Testing & CI
facts.value: GitHub Actions runs Vitest (jsdom) on Node 20; additional workflows for lint, size-limit, test-coverage, locales-coverage, semantic PR titles

facts.label: Code quality tooling
facts.value: ESLint (max-warnings=0), Prettier, tsc typecheck, husky pre-commit + lint-staged

facts.label: Firebase infra config
facts.value: Dedicated firebase-project with firestore.rules, storage.rules, and firestore indexes committed

business.summary: Excalidraw is a bootstrapped, open-source (MIT-licensed) virtual collaborative whiteboard for hand-drawn-style diagrams, launched in 2020 by former Meta engineer Christopher Chedeau and now run by a small team (~6 people) headquartered in Brno, Czech Republic. It operates a freemium model: the core app is free forever, while Excalidraw+ is a paid, seat-based cloud tier (~$6/user/month billed yearly, $7 monthly) targeting teams and enterprises with cloud workspaces, access management, presentations, comments, voice hangouts, and AI features. Its differentiation is deliberate simplicity, a sketch-like aesthetic, privacy-first end-to-end encryption, and no-signup access—serving individual developers plus small-to-mid-market product, engineering, and business teams who want quick informal diagramming without enterprise complexity. It competes with tldraw (an open-source infinite-canvas SDK for developers, VC-backed with ~$14M raised), Figma FigJam (design-team whiteboard bundled into Figma seats), and Miro (the enterprise category leader with ~90-100M users, ~$665M ARR and a $17.5B valuation). Key growth constraints are converting free/open-source users into paid Excalidraw+ subscribers, limited enterprise support capacity given the tiny team, and meeting enterprise auditability/reliability/security demands (addressed via SOC 2 Type II compliance and a DPA) against far larger, better-funded rivals.

facts.label: Company stage & structure
facts.value: Bootstrapped, independent open-source company headquartered in Brno, Czech Republic; runs as an open-source project with a roughly 6-person team, serving a global user base from individual developers to enterprises.

facts.label: Founding & origin
facts.value: Launched January 1, 2020 by Christopher Chedeau, a software engineer at Meta, with a mission of simplicity over feature bloat; grew from ~12,000 users in its first weeks to 350,000 monthly users by 2023, with 110,000+ GitHub stars.

facts.label: Industry / product
facts.value: Virtual collaborative whiteboard for creating hand-drawn-style diagrams, wireframes, and illustrations; open source under the MIT license with end-to-end encryption and no-signup access for secure collaboration.

facts.label: Revenue model & pricing
facts.value: Freemium model with subscription revenue only (no ads, no data monetization): core app free forever, Excalidraw+ paid seat-based cloud tier at ~$6/user/month billed yearly ($7 monthly), 14-day free trial, with education discounts.

facts.label: Paid-tier features & enterprise trust
facts.value: Excalidraw+ adds unlimited cloud scenes/folders, access management, sharing with edit/view rights, presentations, comments, voice hangouts/screensharing, and extended AI; it is SOC 2 Type II compliant with a DPA in place to meet enterprise security/auditability needs.

facts.label: Customer segments & positioning
facts.value: Serves small-to-mid-market companies across product, engineering, and business teams needing quick informal diagramming; competes on simplicity, privacy-first architecture, and zero cost for core features rather than on advanced integrations vs. Miro/Figma.

facts.label: Strategic goal / why they built a company
facts.value: The company was formed around Excalidraw with Excalidraw+ as its first product to address enterprise adoption complaints—easy sharing among co-workers, dependable live collaboration, and data security—that a volunteer-based open-source model couldn't solve.

facts.label: Competitor: tldraw
facts.value: London-based startup founded 2022 by Steve Ruiz; open-source infinite-canvas SDK for developers (used by ClickUp, Padlet, Craft, Jam), closed just under $1M in license deals in its first year, raised a $10M Series A in April 2025 (Lux Capital, Definition), ~$14M total raised.

facts.label: Competitor: Figma FigJam
facts.value: Lightweight, design-centric whiteboard built by Figma for design teams and early-stage ideation; its pricing is bundled into Figma seats and it integrates tightly with Figma Design, though it lacks advanced diagramming depth versus Miro.

facts.label: Competitor: Miro
facts.value: Category-leading enterprise visual-collaboration platform (founded 2011, formerly RealtimeBoard); ~90-100M users across 250,000 organizations (99 of Fortune 100), tiered subscriptions from free to enterprise, estimated ~$665M ARR in 2024 and a $17.5B valuation from its 2022 Series C.

facts.label: Growth bottleneck & market constraint
facts.value: The freemium open-source model creates conversion challenges (most users stay free) and limited enterprise support capacity from a tiny team; the broader whiteboard market is projected to reach ~$8.11B by 2033 (14.3% CAGR), intensifying competition with far larger, better-funded rivals.

temporal.summary: There is a near-term internal team planning meeting on the collaboration roadmap in 2 days, making it the most immediate time pressure. A conference talk submission deadline on the collaboration story falls in 3 weeks, and the next quarterly board update covering the monetization plan is in 6 weeks. Competitive pressure from tldraw's recent funding and aggressive SDK shipping adds urgency to these decisions.

facts.label: Team planning meeting (collaboration roadmap)
facts.value: in 2 days

facts.label: Conference talk submission deadline (collaboration story)
facts.value: in 3 weeks

facts.value: in 6 weeks

facts.value: tldraw just raised a $10M Series A and is shipping a realtime collaboration SDK aggressively

facts.value: Excalidraw+ subscription growth has been flat for two quarters

extractFindings.fact: Language & framework: TypeScript 5.9 + React 19, built with Vite 5; canvas-based whiteboard SPA

Architecture: Yarn workspaces monorepo: excalidraw-app + internal packages (common, element, math, excalidraw, utils, fractional-indexing, laser-pointer); library published as @excalidraw/excalidraw npm package
Internal packages: Modular design with separate packages: common, element, excalidraw, fractional-indexing, laser-pointer, math, utils
Persistence & backend: No custom backend; scenes stored in Firebase Firestore, files in Firebase Storage, both end-to-end encrypted client-side
Real-time collaboration: socket.io-client 4.7.2 used for real-time collab; local-first with idb-keyval and jotai for state
Deployment (self-host): Multi-stage Dockerfile building with Node 24 and serving static build via nginx-alpine; docker-compose exposes port 3000:80
Deployment (hosted): Vercel deploys excalidraw-app/build with CORS/security headers and host-based redirects; install via yarn
Observability: Sentry browser error tracking (@sentry/browser 9.0.1); tracking toggled via VITE_APP env flags, disabled in Docker builds
Testing & CI: GitHub Actions runs Vitest (jsdom) on Node 20; additional workflows for lint, size-limit, test-coverage, locales-coverage, semantic PR titles
Code quality tooling: ESLint (max-warnings=0), Prettier, tsc typecheck, husky pre-commit + lint-staged
Firebase infra config: Dedicated firebase-project with firestore.rules, storage.rules, and firestore indexes committed

Company stage & structure: Bootstrapped, independent open-source company headquartered in Brno, Czech Republic; runs as an open-source project with a roughly 6-person team, serving a global user base from individual developers to enterprises.

Founding & origin: Launched January 1, 2020 by Christopher Chedeau, a software engineer at Meta, with a mission of simplicity over feature bloat; grew from ~12,000 users in its first weeks to 350,000 monthly users by 2023, with 110,000+ GitHub stars.

Industry / product: Virtual collaborative whiteboard for creating hand-drawn-style diagrams, wireframes, and illustrations; open source under the MIT license with end-to-end encryption and no-signup access for secure collaboration.

Revenue model & pricing: Freemium model with subscription revenue only (no ads, no data monetization): core app free forever, Excalidraw+ paid seat-based cloud tier at ~$6/user/month billed yearly ($7 monthly), 14-day free trial, with education discounts.

Paid-tier features & enterprise trust: Excalidraw+ adds unlimited cloud scenes/folders, access management, sharing with edit/view rights, presentations, comments, voice hangouts/screensharing, and extended AI; it is SOC 2 Type II compliant with a DPA in place to meet enterprise security/auditability needs.

Customer segments & positioning: Serves small-to-mid-market companies across product, engineering, and business teams needing quick informal diagramming; competes on simplicity, privacy-first architecture, and zero cost for core features rather than on advanced integrations vs. Miro/Figma.

Strategic goal / why they built a company: The company was formed around Excalidraw with Excalidraw+ as its first product to address enterprise adoption complaints—easy sharing among co-workers, dependable live collaboration, and data security—that a volunteer-based open-source model couldn't solve.

Competitor: tldraw: London-based startup founded 2022 by Steve Ruiz; open-source infinite-canvas SDK for developers (used by ClickUp, Padlet, Craft, Jam), closed just under $1M in license deals in its first year, raised a $10M Series A in April 2025 (Lux Capital, Definition), ~$14M total raised.

Competitor: Figma FigJam: Lightweight, design-centric whiteboard built by Figma for design teams and early-stage ideation; its pricing is bundled into Figma seats and it integrates tightly with Figma Design, though it lacks advanced diagramming depth versus Miro.

Competitor: Miro: Category-leading enterprise visual-collaboration platform (founded 2011, formerly RealtimeBoard); ~90-100M users across 250,000 organizations (99 of Fortune 100), tiered subscriptions from free to enterprise, estimated ~$665M ARR in 2024 and a $17.5B valuation from its 2022 Series C.

Growth bottleneck & market constraint: The freemium open-source model creates conversion challenges (most users stay free) and limited enterprise support capacity from a tiny team; the broader whiteboard market is projected to reach ~$8.11B by 2033 (14.3% CAGR), intensifying competition with far larger, better-funded rivals.
Team planning meeting (collaboration roadmap): in 2 days
Conference talk submission deadline (collaboration story): in 3 weeks
Quarterly board update (monetization plan): in 6 weeks
Competitive context: tldraw just raised a $10M Series A and is shipping a realtime collaboration SDK aggressively
Business backdrop: Excalidraw+ subscription growth has been flat for two quarters

nodes.label: Team can build and operate infra

evidence.fact: Bootstrapped, independent open-source company headquartered in Brno, Czech Republic; runs as an open-source project with a roughly 6-person team, serving a global user base from individual developers to enterprises.
Real-time collaboration: socket.io-client 4.7.2 used for real-time collab; local-first with idb-keyval and jotai for state
Observability: Sentry browser error tracking (@sentry/browser 9.0.1); tracking toggled via VITE_APP env flags, disabled in Docker builds
Business backdrop: Excalidraw+ subscription growth has been flat for two quarters
It is SOC 2 Type II compliant with a DPA in place to meet enterprise security/auditability needs.
Competitive context: tldraw just raised a $10M Series A and is shipping a realtime collaboration SDK aggressively

attacks.rationale: 6-person team with no backend history has no spare capacity; the 2-day roadmap meeting forces a build commitment they cannot staff.
Owning realtime infra raises the reliability burden while observability is only Sentry error tracking — inadequate before enterprise SLAs.
Reimplementing E2E-encrypted Socket.IO collab off excalidraw-room is deep work unlikely to be scoped before the 3-week talk submission.
Growth bottleneck is free-to-paid conversion; backend build competes with that priority goal without proof collab quality gates conversion.
SOC 2 / DPA demands need audited controls and process, not merely owning infra; own-backend claim may not deliver the differentiator before the board update.
